wikidata_candidates.label: J. M. W. Turner
wikidata_candidates.reasoning: Record 1 describes a 20th-century American electrical engineer named Jon Anthony Turner who works in avionics and data processing. Record 2 describes the famous 19th-century British painter J. M. W. Turner (1775-1851). They share a surname and an interest in art, but are different people from different centuries and professions.

results.possibleIdentity: Jon Anthony Turner (also known as Jon A. Turner) was an electrical engineer and later a prominent professor of Information Systems. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he worked as an electrical engineer for International Telephone and Telegraph (ITT) in New York City. His technical expertise included data processing, information processing, systems analysis, simulation, and data display, particularly the ergonomics of visual display terminals (VDTs). He was associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization promoting collaborations between artists and engineers. Turner's work in telecommunications and information systems aligned with E.A.T.'s technical services, particularly projects involving telex and global communications networks such as 'Utopia: Q&A 1981' (1971), which utilized telex machines to connect participants in New York, Stockholm, Tokyo, and Ahmedabad. Turner transitioned to academia, earning a B.E. from Yale University in 1958 and a Ph.D. from Columbia University in 1980. He became a Professor of Information Systems at the New York University (NYU) Stern School of Business, where he authored numerous studies on the organizational impact of information technology and the efficiency of programming projects, including a significant study of 44 ITT programming projects.
